and finally as the **Irn-Bru Scottish Football League**. The SFL also organised two knock-out cup competitions, the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") and the [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defunct association football league in Scotland

Football league

Scottish Football League (SFL)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Founded | 1890 |
| Folded | 2013 |
| Country | [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") |
| [Confederation](/wiki/Geography_of_association_football "Geography of association football") | [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") |
| Last champions | [Partick Thistle](/wiki/Partick_Thistle_F.C. "Partick Thistle F.C.")  ([2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Scottish_Football_League "2012–13 Scottish Football League")) |
| Most championships | [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") (47 titles)[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) |

The **Scottish Football League** (**SFL**) is a defunct league featuring professional and semi-professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") clubs mostly from [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland").[[note 2]](#cite_note-2) From its foundation in 1890 until the breakaway [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") (SPL) was formed in 1998, the SFL was the top level of [football in Scotland](/wiki/Football_in_Scotland "Football in Scotland"). After 1998, the SFL represented levels 2 to 4 of the [Scottish football league system](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system"). In June 2013, the SFL merged with the SPL to form the [Scottish Professional Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League").

The SFL was associated with a [title sponsor](/wiki/Title_sponsor "Title sponsor") from the [1985–86](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_in_Scottish_football "1985–86 in Scottish football") season. As this sponsor changed over the years the league was known in turn as the **Fine Fare League**, **B&Q League**, **Bell's Scottish Football League** and finally as the **Irn-Bru Scottish Football League**. The SFL also organised two knock-out cup competitions, the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") and the [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of Scottish football](/wiki/History_of_Scottish_football "History of Scottish football")

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

Organised football in Scotland began in 1873 with the formation of the [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association") (SFA).[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey3-3) During the next 15 years or so, clubs would play [friendly matches](/wiki/Friendly_match "Friendly match"), [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") ties and local cup (e.g. [Glasgow Cup](/wiki/Glasgow_Cup "Glasgow Cup") or [East of Scotland Shield](/wiki/East_of_Scotland_Shield "East of Scotland Shield")) ties.[[1]](#cite_note-Crampsey3-3) [The Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League"), initially containing clubs from the [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") and [Midlands](/wiki/Midlands_(England) "Midlands (England)") of England, was formed in 1888.[[2]](#cite_note-Crampsey5-4) This had been done in response to the professionalisation of football in England in 1885, with the regular diet of league fixtures replacing the haphazard arrangement of friendlies.[[2]](#cite_note-Crampsey5-4) Many Scottish players, known as the [Scotch Professors](/wiki/Scotch_Professors "Scotch Professors"), moved to the English league clubs to receive the relatively high salaries on offer.[[3]](#cite_note-Crampsey6-5)

This prompted Scottish clubs into thinking about forming their own league. In March 1890, the secretary of [Renton](/wiki/Renton_F.C. "Renton F.C.") wrote to thirteen other clubs inviting them to discuss the organisation of a league.[[3]](#cite_note-Crampsey6-5) All of the clubs accepted the invitation, except [Queen's Park](/wiki/Queen%27s_Park_F.C. "Queen's Park F.C.") and [Clyde](/wiki/Clyde_F.C. "Clyde F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-Crampsey7-6) Amateur club Queen's Park, who were the oldest organised club in Scotland and had played a key role in the development of football, were opposed to the league because it would lead to professionalism and eliminate many of the smaller clubs.[[4]](#cite_note-Crampsey7-6) These concerns were to prove well-founded, as six of the founder members would leave the league before 1900.[[4]](#cite_note-Crampsey7-6)

The Scottish Football League (SFL) was inaugurated on 30 April 1890.[[5]](#cite_note-Crampsey13-7) The first season of competition, [1890–91](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_Scottish_Football_League "1890–91 Scottish Football League"), commenced with 11 clubs because [St Bernard's](/wiki/St_Bernard%27s_F.C. "St Bernard's F.C.") were not elected.[[5]](#cite_note-Crampsey13-7) The eleven original clubs in membership were [Abercorn](/wiki/Abercorn_F.C. "Abercorn F.C."), [Cambuslang](/wiki/Cambuslang_F.C. "Cambuslang F.C."), [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C."), [Cowlairs](/wiki/Cowlairs_F.C. "Cowlairs F.C."), [Dumbarton](/wiki/Dumbarton_F.C. "Dumbarton F.C."), [Heart of Midlothian](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C."), [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C."), [Renton](/wiki/Renton_F.C. "Renton F.C."), [St Mirren](/wiki/St_Mirren_F.C. "St Mirren F.C."), [Third Lanark](/wiki/Third_Lanark_A.C. "Third Lanark A.C.") and [Vale of Leven](/wiki/Vale_of_Leven_F.C. "Vale of Leven F.C.").[[6]](#cite_note-8) Renton were expelled after five games of the 1890–91 season[[7]](#cite_note-Crampsey20-9) for playing against [St Bernard's](/wiki/St_Bernard%27s_F.C. "St Bernard's F.C."), who had been found guilty of concealed professionalism. Renton raised an action against the SFA in the [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session") and won, which meant that their SFA and SFL memberships were restored.[[7]](#cite_note-Crampsey20-9)

In the [1890–91](/wiki/1890%E2%80%9391_in_Scottish_football "1890–91 in Scottish football") season, Rangers and Dumbarton were level at the top of the league on 29 points.[[7]](#cite_note-Crampsey20-9) The teams drew 2–2 in a [play-off](/wiki/Play-off "Play-off") match, but no further thought had been given to separating teams by another method and the championship was shared.[[7]](#cite_note-Crampsey20-9) [Goal average](/wiki/Goal_average "Goal average") was introduced for the [1921–22](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_in_Scottish_football "1921–22 in Scottish football") season and replaced by goal difference for the [1971–72](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_in_Scottish_football "1971–72 in Scottish football") season.[[8]](#cite_note-goals-10)

### Split into divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Split into divisions")]

The league proved to be highly successful, and in 1893, a second tier was formed by the inclusion of a number of clubs previously in the [Scottish Football Alliance](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Alliance "Scottish Football Alliance"). Promotion was initially based on a ballot of clubs; automatic promotion was not introduced until 1922.

The onset of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") saw Division Two but not Division One being suspended, not restarting again until 1921 when the [Central Football League](/wiki/Central_Football_League "Central Football League") was absorbed as a new division with automatic promotion.

### Division Three

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Division Three")]

In 1923, the League decided to introduce a third tier. The [Western League](/w/index.php?title=Western_Football_League_(Scotland)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Western Football League (Scotland) (page does not exist)") was used as its backbone[[9]](#cite_note-cdiv-11) but the new set-up lasted only three years before it collapsed under heavy financial losses. From 1926 until 1946, the League reverted to two divisions with many of the third tier clubs joining the second iteration of Scottish Football Alliance.[[10]](#cite_note-12)

Post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") reforms saw the League resume with three divisions, renamed 'A', 'B' and 'C' with the last section also including [reserve sides](/wiki/Reserve_team "Reserve team").[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-14) In 1947, league championship trophies were introduced for the first time for the top two divisions, until then only flags had been presented to the winners. The new trophies were engraved with the names of all previous winners.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

In 1949, the 'C' Division was expanded to two sections – South-East (North-East from 1950) and South-West. The withdrawal of the reserves from 'C' Division in 1955 saw a return to two divisions with the 5 first teams in Division C being given automatic promotion.[[9]](#cite_note-cdiv-11)[[14]](#cite_note-16) There were then 18 clubs in Division A and 19 in Division B. In 1956 the divisions were renamed Division 1 and Division 2.

[Clydebank](/wiki/Clydebank_F.C._(1965) "Clydebank F.C. (1965)") were elected to Division Two as the 20th club in 1966, but following the demise of [Third Lanark](/wiki/Third_Lanark_A.C. "Third Lanark A.C.") in 1967, Division Two kept operating with just 19 clubs; the situation would not be corrected until the next change of format, which happened in 1975.

This change of structure split the league into three divisions, Premier, First, and Second Divisions. This permitted more frequent fixtures between the top clubs; the expectation was that meant greater revenue for them, and it was hoped it would stimulate greater interest, at a time when attendance at league matches had dropped alarmingly. One year before the restructuring, a new club entered the league, [Meadowbank Thistle](/wiki/Meadowbank_Thistle "Meadowbank Thistle") (which would eventually become [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston_F.C. "Livingston F.C.")). This three-divisional structure of 38 clubs continued until 1994.

### Four-division period and SPL split

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Four-division period and SPL split")]

After a couple of decades, in 1994 the league again reorganised (following an attempted 'Super League' breakaway by the top clubs in 1992)[[15]](#cite_note-17) with four divisions of 10 teams, as [Highland League](/wiki/Highland_Football_League "Highland Football League") clubs [Inverness Caledonian Thistle](/wiki/Inverness_Caledonian_Thistle_F.C. "Inverness Caledonian Thistle F.C.") (a merger of two existing clubs in the city) and [Ross County](/wiki/Ross_County_F.C. "Ross County F.C.") were elected to round out the league. At the same time, the SFL adopted the system of [three points for a win](/wiki/Three_points_for_a_win "Three points for a win"). In 1998 the Premier Division clubs split from the league to form the [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") (SPL).[[16]](#cite_note-18) The remaining leagues, of ten clubs each, kept their names and the Premier Division was not reconstituted, leaving First, Second and Third Divisions. Part of the agreement was that the SPL would expand to 12 clubs in 2000. The SFL then took in two new members to replace the two clubs lost to the SPL; Highland League clubs [Elgin City](/wiki/Elgin_City_F.C. "Elgin City F.C.") and [Peterhead](/wiki/Peterhead_F.C. "Peterhead F.C.") were admitted, increasing the total number of clubs in the Scottish football league system to 42.

From 2005, the SFL determined a [promotion and relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") place between each division according to [playoffs](/wiki/Playoffs "Playoffs") between four clubs.[[17]](#cite_note-promotion-19) The playoffs were contested between the ninth placed (second bottom) club in the higher division and the second, third and fourth placed clubs in the lower division.[[17]](#cite_note-promotion-19) It was therefore possible for a team finishing fourth in the Second Division or Third Division to be promoted, rather than the clubs finishing immediately above them in the standings.[[17]](#cite_note-promotion-19) It was also possible for the ninth placed club to retain their position in the higher division.[[17]](#cite_note-promotion-19)

> Our review of non-financial matters indicate an organisation which is not wholly suited to the modern-day business environment, or the size of the league.

— Pannell Kerr Foster, *BBC Sport website*

In March 2007, a self-conducted review found the league to be three times more expensive to run than equivalent leagues in England, with a report stating the league structure was "outdated": the report found that the [Football Conference](/wiki/Football_Conference "Football Conference") had four employees looking after 68 clubs, while the SFL had 14 people running leagues with just 30 clubs.[[18]](#cite_note-review-20)

### Merger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Merger")]

No clubs were relegated from the bottom end of the Scottish Football League,[[17]](#cite_note-promotion-19) although there were changes of membership due to clubs going out of business.[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-annan-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24) The Scottish Football Association were keen for a pyramid system to be instituted. SFL First Division clubs wanted to gain a greater share of the media revenue generated by the SPL.[[23]](#cite_note-merger-25) In 2013, both the SPL and SFL voted in favour of merging to form the new [Scottish Professional Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League").[[23]](#cite_note-merger-25) The number of divisions and clubs in each division remained the same, but there were changes to the financial distribution model.[[23]](#cite_note-merger-25) The first club to be promoted to the SPFL from the new [Lowland League](/wiki/Lowland_Football_League "Lowland Football League") via playoffs was [Edinburgh City](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C. "Edinburgh City F.C.") in 2016 at the expense of [East Stirlingshire](/wiki/East_Stirlingshire "East Stirlingshire"), while in 2019 [Cove Rangers](/wiki/Cove_Rangers_F.C. "Cove Rangers F.C.") were the first [Highland League](/wiki/Highland_Football_League "Highland Football League") champions to go up, replacing [Berwick Rangers](/wiki/Berwick_Rangers_F.C. "Berwick Rangers F.C.").

## Cup competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cup competitions")]

Main articles: [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") and [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup")

The Scottish Football League organised two knock-out cup competitions: the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") and the [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup"). The League Cup was established in 1946, succeeding the wartime Southern League Cup. Unlike the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup"), organised by the [Scottish Football Association](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Association "Scottish Football Association"), the League Cup was only open to Scottish Football League clubs. Scottish Premier League clubs continued to participate in the League Cup after the top tier clubs broke away in 1998. Until the mid-1990s, the competition winner was eligible to participate in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"), but this was discontinued due to a loss of European places.

The Scottish Challenge Cup was instituted in 1990, to celebrate the League's centenary.[[24]](#cite_note-26) The Challenge Cup was only open to Scottish Football League clubs outside the top division of the [Scottish football league system](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system"). From 2011, two [Highland Football League](/wiki/Highland_Football_League "Highland Football League") clubs were allowed to participate each year in the Challenge Cup, to give a round number of 32 clubs participating.[[25]](#cite_note-new_ramsdens-27) Both the League Cup and the Challenge Cup continued under the auspices of the [Scottish Professional Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League").

## League sponsorship and media rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: League sponsorship and media rights")]

From 1985, the League accepted sponsorships for its main competition.[[26]](#cite_note-Crampsey218-28) Below is a list of sponsors and the League's name under their sponsorship:

* 1985–1988: [Fine Fare](/wiki/Fine_Fare "Fine Fare") (Fine Fare League)[[26]](#cite_note-Crampsey218-28)
* 1988–1992: [B&Q](/wiki/B%26Q "B&Q") (B&Q League)[[26]](#cite_note-Crampsey218-28)
* 1992–1994: *No sponsor*
* 1994–1998: [Bell's whisky](/wiki/Bell%27s_whisky "Bell's whisky") (Bell's League)[[27]](#cite_note-29)
* 1998–1999: *No sponsor*
* 1999–2006: [Bell's whisky](/wiki/Bell%27s_whisky "Bell's whisky") (Bell's League)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-irn-bru_2007-31)
* 2006–2007: *No sponsor*[[29]](#cite_note-irn-bru_2007-31)
* 2007–2013: [Irn-Bru](/wiki/Irn-Bru "Irn-Bru") (Irn-Bru League)[[29]](#cite_note-irn-bru_2007-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)

The League's cup competitions have had different sponsors, with the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") first attracting sponsorship in 1979.[[32]](#cite_note-34) The competition was revamped in 1984, adopting a straight knock-out format, when [Skol Lager](/wiki/Skol_Lager "Skol Lager") began its sponsorship.[[33]](#cite_note-35) [The Co-operative Insurance](/wiki/The_Co-operative_Insurance "The Co-operative Insurance") company sponsored the League Cup for 12 seasons, until the [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Scottish_League_Cup "2010–11 Scottish League Cup") competition.[[34]](#cite_note-36) It has since been sponsored by the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), under the name of the *Scottish Communities League Cup*.[[35]](#cite_note-37) The [Scottish Challenge Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Challenge_Cup "Scottish Challenge Cup") was created by League sponsor B&Q in 1990, but it was cancelled in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_in_Scottish_football "1998–99 in Scottish football") due to a lack of sponsorship.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Since 2008, the Challenge Cup has attracted its own sponsor, with [BBC Alba](/wiki/BBC_Alba "BBC Alba") and Ramsdens providing support.[[25]](#cite_note-new_ramsdens-27)

Before 1979, the major source of revenue to Scottish Football League clubs, apart from their attendances, was from the [football pools](/wiki/Football_pool "Football pool").[[37]](#cite_note-crampsey259-39) In the year ended 31 March 1983, the pools generated £1.08 million out of a total of £1.46 million.[[38]](#cite_note-40) By 1990, this source of income had been overtaken by revenue from sponsorships and television rights.[[37]](#cite_note-crampsey259-39) As of 1990, 75% of these central revenues were split equally between the 38 member clubs, with the remaining 25% allocated according to each club's position in the league ladder.[[37]](#cite_note-crampsey259-39) During the 1970s and 1980s, the main terrestrial television companies ([STV](/wiki/STV_(TV_network) "STV (TV network)") and [BBC Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Scotland "BBC Scotland")) produced shows (*[Scotsport](/wiki/Scotsport "Scotsport")* and *[Sportscene](/wiki/Sportscene "Sportscene")* respectively) containing highlights of league matches.[[39]](#cite_note-crampsey198-41) The revenues from these broadcasts were relatively small, with the companies paying less than £1000 per match in the mid-1970s.[[39]](#cite_note-crampsey198-41) [BBC Radio Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Scotland "BBC Radio Scotland") had exclusive rights for live radio coverage of matches at this time,[[40]](#cite_note-42) with independent stations such as [Radio Clyde](/wiki/Radio_Clyde "Radio Clyde") providing coverage via score updates and analysis.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

The first live television broadcast of a Scottish league match was not until April 1986.[[42]](#cite_note-hearts_86-44) Earlier in the [1985–86](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_in_Scottish_football "1985–86 in Scottish football") season, there had been no television coverage at all due to a dispute between the League and the broadcasters.[[42]](#cite_note-hearts_86-44) The birth of satellite broadcaster [British Sky Broadcasting](/wiki/British_Sky_Broadcasting "British Sky Broadcasting") (Sky TV) changed the situation significantly.[[43]](#cite_note-keys-45) As ITV had an exclusive contract for live coverage of games in [the English league](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League"), the first match shown on Sky was a [Scottish Premier Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Premier_Division "Scottish Football League Premier Division") match between [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") and [Dundee United](/wiki/Dundee_United_F.C. "Dundee United F.C.") in April 1991.[[43]](#cite_note-keys-45) A year later, the top division English clubs formed a breakaway [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), signing an exclusive television contract with Sky. Live Scottish Premier Division games were shown on STV during the 1990s, but the top division clubs formed the breakaway [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League") in 1998 and signed an exclusive broadcast contract with Sky.

This left the remaining Scottish Football League clubs without live coverage, although STV continued to show highlights of [First Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_First_Division "Scottish Football League First Division") games in their *Football First* show. [Scots Gaelic](/wiki/Scots_Gaelic "Scots Gaelic") channel [BBC Alba](/wiki/BBC_Alba "BBC Alba") provided coverage of Scottish Football League games, including the Challenge Cup, from its launch in 2008.[[25]](#cite_note-new_ramsdens-27) Live coverage on English language channels returned to Scottish Football League games in the 2012–13 season, as Sky and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_(UK) "ESPN (UK)") agreed contracts to show [Third Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Third_Division "Scottish Football League Third Division") matches involving [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.").[[44]](#cite_note-sky_deal-46)[[45]](#cite_note-espn_deal-47) These arrangements secured revenues that had been under threat due to the [financial collapse of Rangers](/wiki/Administration_and_liquidation_of_The_Rangers_Football_Club_Plc "Administration and liquidation of The Rangers Football Club Plc").[[44]](#cite_note-sky_deal-46)[[45]](#cite_note-espn_deal-47)

## Past winners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Past winners")]

Main articles: [List of Scottish football champions](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_football_champions "List of Scottish football champions"), [List of winners of the Scottish Championship and predecessors](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_the_Scottish_Championship_and_predecessors "List of winners of the Scottish Championship and predecessors"), and [List of winners of the Scottish League One and predecessors](/wiki/List_of_winners_of_the_Scottish_League_One_and_predecessors "List of winners of the Scottish League One and predecessors")

When the Scottish Football League was established in 1890, all of the clubs played in just one division. In 1893 the Scottish Football League absorbed many clubs from the [Scottish Football Alliance](/wiki/Scottish_Football_Alliance "Scottish Football Alliance") and had enough clubs to form another division. The existing division was renamed Division One and the new division was called Division Two. Nevertheless, [promotion and relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") between the top two divisions was not automatic until [1921–22](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_in_Scottish_football "1921–22 in Scottish football") when Division Two was brought back after a hiatus provoked by [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") which affected only that division; hence some teams of the era winning Division Two twice in a row before being promoted, and some Division Two winners being never promoted at all.

A third tier of Scottish league football was first established in the [1923–24](/wiki/1923%E2%80%9324_in_Scottish_football "1923–24 in Scottish football") season, but it only lasted for two full seasons due to financial difficulties. A third tier league (called Division C) was reintroduced in [1946–47](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_in_Scottish_football "1946–47 in Scottish football"). Division C, which also included [reserve teams](/wiki/Reserve_team "Reserve team") of higher division clubs, operated as a national competition for the first three seasons but thereafter it was split into two regional sections. During this period only full-strength clubs (not reserve teams) were promoted if they finished as champions. The two-division tier was abolished after the [1954–55](/wiki/1954%E2%80%9355_in_Scottish_football "1954–55 in Scottish football") season.

Since the [1975–76](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_in_Scottish_football "1975–76 in Scottish football") season there has been a third tier, known as the [Second Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Second_Division "Scottish Football League Second Division"). The top tier became the [Premier Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Premier_Division "Scottish Football League Premier Division") and the second tier became known as the First Division. A fourth tier, known as the [Third Division](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_Third_Division "Scottish Football League Third Division"), was introduced in 1994. The last major change within the [Scottish football league system](/wiki/Scottish_football_league_system "Scottish football league system") was in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_in_Scottish_football "1998–99 in Scottish football"), when the Premier Division clubs broke away from the Scottish Football League to form the [Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League"). The remaining Scottish Football League divisions continued as before.

## Scottish Football League XI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Scottish Football League XI")]

Main article: [Scottish Football League XI](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_XI "Scottish Football League XI")

The SFL was the organising body of the [Scottish Football League XI](/wiki/Scottish_Football_League_XI "Scottish Football League XI"), a select side which represented the SFL in matches against other leagues, including the [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League"), the [League of Ireland](/wiki/League_of_Ireland "League of Ireland"), the [Northern Irish league](/wiki/NIFL_Premiership "NIFL Premiership") and the Italian [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A").[[76]](#cite_note-79) These matches began in 1892, soon after the foundation of the SFL.[[77]](#cite_note-Crampsey245-80) Before the Second World War, inter-league matches were only second in importance to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_men%27s_national_football_team "Scotland men's national football team") international matches. After the war, however, the inter-league matches became less important as European club football was instituted and clubs withdrew players due to fixture congestion.[[78]](#cite_note-Crampsey254-81) The last inter-league match was played in 1980, while a Scottish Football League XI was last selected in 1990 for a match against the [Scotland national team](/wiki/Scotland_men%27s_national_football_team "Scotland men's national football team") to mark the centenary of the SFL.[[79]](#cite_note-82)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_Football_League&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** The Scottish Football League existed between 1890 and 2013, and did not contain the top division of Scottish league football between 1998 and 2013 ([Scottish Premier League](/wiki/Scottish_Premier_League "Scottish Premier League")). For a complete record of clubs that have won Scottish league championships, see [list of Scottish football champions](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_football_champions "List of Scottish football champions").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** One club, [Berwick Rangers](/wiki/Berwick_Rangers_F.C. "Berwick Rangers F.C."), is based in the town of [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed"), which is located approximately 4 km south of the [Anglo-Scottish border](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_border "Anglo-Scottish border").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** Helensburgh gained the most points, but no title was awarded as they were the only club to complete their fixtures.
